text.title: TDD Workflow and CI Discipline
text.summary: Red-green-refactor loop, commit discipline, test-suite speed rules, and mutation testing for validating test quality.

rule.statement: Write the failing test before writing any production code. Never write production code to pass a test that does not yet exist. Each red-green-refactor cycle produces three commits: (1) failing test, (2) minimum code to pass, (3) refactor.
rule.rationale: Tests written after the fact verify the implementation that already exists, not the behavior that was intended. Pre-written tests catch intent drift immediately and make the refactor step verifiable.

rule.statement: Refactor in a separate commit from feature changes. The refactor commit changes structure without changing behavior; the test suite must remain green before and after.
rule.rationale: Combining refactor and feature in one commit makes the diff unreadable for review. Reviewers cannot distinguish structural change from behavioral change. Separate commits let each be reviewed on its own terms.

example.code:
# Commit 1: failing test git add tests/test_order.py git commit -m "test: add failing test for order validation"
# Commit 2: minimum passing code git add src/orders.py git commit -m "feat: add order validation (min passing impl)"
# Commit 3: refactor git add src/orders.py git commit -m "refactor: extract validate_line_items() from validate_order()"

rule.statement: Keep the fast test suite under 10 minutes. Split into fast (unit, no I/O) and slow (integration, DB, external services) suites. Run fast on every push; slow on nightly or PR merge.
rule.rationale: A suite that takes 30+ minutes kills the CI feedback loop. Developers push less frequently, decouple tests from code, and eventually skip them. The 10-minute rule forces the fast/slow split.

section.title: Mutation Testing

rule.statement: Run mutation testing (mutmut for Python, Stryker for JS/TS) on the critical path monthly. A mutation score below 70% means tests are not actually verifying behavior. Fix the tests, not the mutation score.
rule.rationale: Coverage percentage measures which lines were executed; mutation score measures which behaviors were verified. A test that executes code without asserting its output counts as coverage but not as a test.

section.reference: pre-commit config enforcing ruff + pytest fast suite + no test skips
